title: Updating and Removing Software
description: Keep your system patched and clean. Learn to upgrade, remove orphaned packages, and manage dependencies.

slides.title: Keeping Software Fresh: `upgrade` vs `full-upgrade` 🔄
slides.content:
# Applying the Actual Updates

Remember: `apt update` only refreshes the list. To actually install newer versions of software, you need to **upgrade**.

### `sudo apt upgrade`
- Upgrades all installed packages to their latest versions.
- **Never removes packages**. If an upgrade requires removing something, it will be **held back**.

### `sudo apt full-upgrade` (or `dist-upgrade`)
- Upgrades everything, **even if it means removing conflicting packages**.
- Necessary for major system version upgrades (e.g., Ubuntu 22.04 to 24.04).

**Hacker Routine:** You should run this weekly to patch security vulnerabilities.
```bash
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
```

slides.title: Upgrade System
slides.content: Simulate an upgrade (list what would be done) without actually doing it.

quizData.question: Which command will NEVER remove an existing package?
quizData.explanation: `apt upgrade` is conservative and will hold back packages rather than remove dependencies.

slides.title: Removing Software: `remove` vs `purge` 🗑️
slides.content:
# Getting Rid of Bloat

Unused tools take up space and, more importantly, **increase attack surface**. If you don't need it, remove it.

### `sudo apt remove package_name`
- Deletes the binaries and libraries.
- **Leaves behind configuration files** in `/etc/`.

### `sudo apt purge package_name`
- Deletes the binaries AND **configuration files**.
- This is the clean way to uninstall. Always use `purge` unless you plan to reinstall later and want to keep settings.

### Remove Unnecessary Dependencies:
When you install a tool, it pulls in libraries. When you remove the tool, those libraries might become **orphaned** (no longer needed).

```bash
sudo apt autoremove
```
This safely removes all automatically installed packages that are no longer required. **Run this monthly to save disk space.**

slides.title: Check for Cruft
slides.content: See what orphaned packages are taking up space.

quizData.question: You want to completely wipe `apache2` including its config files. Which command?
quizData.explanation: `purge` removes the package and its associated configuration files.

slides.title: The APT Cache: Clean It Up 🧹
slides.content:
# Free Up Gigabytes of Space

Every time you `apt install`, the downloaded `.deb` file is stored in `/var/cache/apt/archives/`. Over time, this cache can grow to **multiple gigabytes**.

### `sudo apt clean`
- Deletes **all** cached `.deb` files.
- Frees up maximum space.

### `sudo apt autoclean`
- Deletes only **obsolete** cached files (old versions you'll never use).
- Keeps the current version in cache.

**Hacker Tip:** If you are on a live USB with limited space, `apt clean` is a lifesaver.

### Bonus: List All Installed Packages
```bash
apt list --installed
```
Or search for a specific one:
```bash
apt list --installed | grep nmap
```

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Holding Packages Back

Sometimes an update breaks your custom tool. You can 'pin' or 'hold' a package to prevent it from being upgraded.

```bash
sudo apt-mark hold metasploit-framework
```

To unhold:
```bash
sudo apt-mark unhold metasploit-framework
```
